finding_title: Shared step opportunities reduce automation maintenance burden
reasoning: Analysis of all 17 feature files identified 12 recurring step patterns (e.g., 'Given clause library version is pinned to 2.1', 'When case status transitions to...', 'Then audit log records'). These steps appear 3-15 times across features. Factoring into shared library will improve maintainability and reduce duplication.
recommendation: Create shared step library in Behave conftest.py with 12 reusable step definitions. Document parameter syntax for case status, timeline, audit log events. Estimated effort: 20 hours shared library development; 30% reduction in future step maintenance.

reasoning: Monte Carlo simulation for swarm emergent behavior (10,000 trials), distributed consensus voting simulation, and C2 link loss scenarios require computationally intensive test infrastructure not typical of BDD automation. Estimated execution time 900-1200 seconds per test. Standard CI/CD pipelines will timeout or consume excessive resources.
recommendation: Segregate swarm simulation scenarios into separate 'simulation' suite executed on dedicated hardware (GPU-accelerated compute nodes). Consider containerized test environment (Docker + Kubernetes) for parallel test execution. Alternative: use stubbed/mocked simulation results for fast-feedback CI; reserve full-fidelity simulation for nightly/weekly runs.

reasoning: Latency-critical requirements (geofence RTL < 100ms, override < 50ms, kill-switch < 350ms) cannot be reliably tested via pure software mock. Software mock latencies are non-deterministic (OS context switches, CPU load). These scenarios require actual embedded hardware or real-time simulation (FPGA, RTX). No amount of test code can substitute for true latency measurement.
recommendation: Implement hardware-in-the-loop (HIL) test bench for latency-critical paths. Candidate frameworks: NI LabVIEW, dSPACE, MathWorks HIL Blockset. Integrate with Behave via serial/Ethernet client binding (not native Gherkin). Estimate: 200 hours for HIL infrastructure setup; latency assertions in Behave verified against HIL measurements.

finding_title: UK sovereign infrastructure constraints affect framework selection
reasoning: Specification implies UK sovereign cloud (Google Cloud Storage with geo-redundancy, CAA Secure Archive). Standard open-source Gherkin frameworks (Cucumber, Behave, SpecFlow) assume public cloud (AWS, Azure) or on-prem deployment. GCS integration requires custom bindings. MOD classified trials require air-gap or classified cloud (e.g., UK IL5 equivalent). Dual-framework strategy (Behave for unclassified, isolated instance for classified) increases complexity.
recommendation: Recommendation 1: Adopt Behave (Python) as primary framework. Justification: Python has strong GCS SDK (google-cloud-storage); classification-aware feature file plugins feasible. Recommendation 2: Create abstraction layer for artifact storage (interface abstraction, swappable GCS/local-storage backend) to support air-gapped classified trials. Recommendation 3: Plan separate test instances (public Workbench + classified Workbench) with shared step library but separate execution contexts.

finding_title: Panel deliberation scenarios resist full automation
reasoning: Panel deliberation requires human judgment, discussion, and subjective override decisions (e.g., 'John Smith overrides AI flag because he found ConOps evidence'). Cannot be fully automated; too much domain expertise and contextual reasoning required. Current scenarios assume deterministic outcomes (APPROVE_WITH_CONDITIONS, REFUSE), but real panel decisions involve committee votes, dissent, escalation negotiations.
recommendation: Adopt hybrid automation model for panel features: (1) Automate setup/teardown (case briefing, evidence assembly). (2) Manual testing for deliberation path (panel meets in-person, records decision via form/template). (3) Automate decision record validation (database assertions on decision object, audit log integrity). (4) Consider optional 'recorded deliberation replay' test mode where historical panel decisions are replayed to verify outcome consistency. Estimate: 40% automation coverage (setup + validation), 60% manual (deliberation logic).

finding_title: ML model forensics require specialized test tooling
reasoning: Scenario 'Post-incident model decision reconstruction' requires ML model inference replay, SHAP/LIME explainability, saliency maps. Standard Gherkin assertion libraries cannot inspect neural network decision boundaries. Requires specialized ML testing framework (e.g., deeptest, TensorFlow Model Audit, PYNVML for GPU profiling). Integration with Behave requires custom step definition that wraps Python ML libraries.
recommendation: Create dedicated ML forensics Behave plugin (Python module wrapping TensorFlow/PyTorch + SHAP). Step definitions should accept model checkpoint + input feature vector, output decision reconstruction + explainability artifacts. Test data: use synthetic training/validation splits to ensure off-distribution detection scenarios are testable. Integrate with CI/CD GPU queue (model inference is computationally expensive).

finding_title: Coverage gap: RF kill-switch validation requires lab-only test environment
reasoning: Independent flight termination RF kill-switch test (915 MHz transmission) cannot be executed in standard office/cloud CI/CD pipeline. Requires Faraday cage or RF-isolated lab. Cannot mock RF propagation realistically (wireless link loss is non-deterministic). Current scenarios assume 'lab-only test' but no explicit mention of test environment segregation.
recommendation: Create explicit test environment classification in Gherkin: @lab-only tag for RF/hardware-dependent scenarios. Segregate from standard CI/CD (they will fail in office). Establish dedicated RF test lab with Faraday cage, RF signal generator, aircraft receiver mockup. Scenario execution: quarterly validation (not on every commit). Document lab access procedure and safety protocols (RF exposure limits per FCC). Backup strategy: partner with aircraft manufacturer for HIL test bench access.

finding_title: GDPR compliance testing absent from feature suite
reasoning: Feature F19 mentions 'DPIA' and 'GDPR compliance' in scenario text, but no Gherkin scenarios explicitly test GDPR requirements (right to access, erasure, portability, consent withdrawal). Feature is descriptive but not testable. GDPR compliance is high-stakes regulatory requirement; gaps could expose CAA to enforcement action.
recommendation: Add dedicated feature file 'features/governance/gdpr_compliance.feature' with scenarios for: (1) Right to Access (user requests flight data, system returns within 30 days). (2) Right to Erasure (user requests deletion post-trial, system confirms removal from archives except legal holds). (3) Consent Management (community member withdraws feedback consent, system redacts from lessons-learned). (4) Data Minimization (system enforces retention schedule: flight data purged after 5 years unless incident-related). Each scenario should assert both positive (requirement met) and negative (breach detected/logged).

finding_title: Emergent behavior test oracle problem unsolved
reasoning: Scenario 'Emergent separation loss collision analysis' claims 'No path to zero-separation collision' via formal verification. But scenario does not specify HOW to verify this claim programmatically. Formal methods (model checking, SAT solving) require specialized tools (TLA+, Alloy, Z3) and expertise. Standard test automation frameworks assume deterministic execution; formal verification is off-equilibrium problem space.
recommendation: Acknowledge test oracle limitation: establish clear boundary between 'executable Gherkin scenarios' and 'formal verification artifacts'. Gherkin scenarios should assert the OUTCOME (formal proof accepted/rejected), not the proof itself. Recommendation: modify scenario as follows: 'When formal verification model of swarm consensus is submitted / Then verification tool (TLA+ or equivalent) accepts model with 0 collision traces in 10M simulation runs'. This makes test oracle clear: tool output (pass/fail) is the source of truth, not Gherkin assertion logic.

finding_title: Applicant feedback segregation requires careful step design
feature_file: Not present in current feature set (should be F25: Operator-Private Feedback vs Regulator Decision Record)
reasoning: Grace's goals and existing features (F25, F13) mention separation of applicant feedback from binding regulatory decisions. Current feature set does not include scenarios for this segregation. Feature file gap: scenarios for feedback-to-decision boundary are missing. This is material to 'defensible approval' goal because audit trails must show feedback (non-binding) vs decisions (binding) clearly separated.
recommendation: Create feature file 'features/panel_review/feedback_decision_segregation.feature' with scenarios: (1) Applicant submits clarification request (non-binding feedback). (2) Case officer updates feedback status. (3) Feedback does NOT appear in final decision record (only in feedback log). (4) Decision record references feedback but does not include feedback text (immutable separation). (5) Audit trail shows feedback timeline separately from decision timeline. Estimated effort: 6-8 hours for feature file + step definitions.

finding_title: Cypress vs Behave split increases framework maintenance
reasoning: Current automation design calls for mixed Cypress (front-end UI) + Behave (back-end service + API). This split requires maintaining two runtime environments, two step definition libraries, and cross-framework data-sharing. Team expertise must span TypeScript/JavaScript (Cypress) and Python (Behave). Integration testing between UI and API becomes non-trivial (Cypress cannot natively interact with Behave test data fixtures).
recommendation: Consolidation strategy: Adopt Behave + Selenium (Python only) instead of Cypress. Justification: Single language (Python), shared test data fixtures, unified CI/CD pipeline. Trade-off: Playwright (TypeScript) offers superior performance vs Selenium; but cost of maintaining TypeScript + Python outweighs speed benefit. Alternative: if UI performance critical, use Behave + Playwright via Python bindings (playwright-python library). Phased approach: Phase 1 (Behave for API/service layers), Phase 2 (add Selenium for UI), Phase 3 (optional Playwright migration if UI performance becomes bottleneck).
